parents.father_research_notes: First Governor of Connecticut; sailed with Thomas Hooker 1633.
First Governor of Connecticut; sailed with Thomas Hooker 1633.

church_roles.notes: Ordained the day the church was organized; preached his own ordination sermon

notable.notable_blurb: The greatest poet of Puritan America

cross_connections.display_label: married his daughter Keziah, then a Hooker descendant
was his grandson and Yale's president

narrative_blocks.header: He is reckoned the finest Puritan poet
narrative_blocks.body: Edward Taylor wrote intricate devotional poetry in the metaphysical vein but forbade its publication in his lifetime. His manuscripts were discovered at Yale in 1937, and he is now regarded as the greatest poet of Puritan America.

narrative_blocks.header: He kept one frontier pulpit fifty-eight years
narrative_blocks.body: A Harvard graduate of 1671, Taylor ministered to the frontier town of Westfield, Massachusetts, for fifty-eight years and doctored his congregation besides. He had left Leicestershire in 1668 rather than conform to the English church.

narrative_blocks.header: His grandson became president of Yale
narrative_blocks.body: Taylor's daughter Keziah married Rev. Isaac Stiles, and their son Ezra Stiles rose to the presidency of Yale. Through Isaac Stiles's later marriage to Esther Hooker, Taylor's family brushed the line of Thomas Hooker.

burial.plot_notes: Table stone, Ancient Burying Ground. Name on Founders Monument. Tombstone erroneously gives Mar 1, 1654; Winthrop letter Jan 9, 1654 confirms recent death.

cross_connections.display_label: was his Griffin shipmate and Fundamental Orders co-author
his granddaughter; she married Yale's founder James Pierpont
bore the Haynes name and married into the Hooker line
governed both New Haven and Connecticut colonies

narrative_blocks.header: Sailed with Thomas Hooker — became first governor
narrative_blocks.body: He and Rev. Thomas Hooker were close friends in England. Both emigrated in 1633 aboard the Griffin, settling at Newtowne (Cambridge). When Hooker led the migration to Connecticut in 1636, Haynes followed — becoming the first governor of the new Connecticut Colony in 1639.

narrative_blocks.header: First governor — eight terms — never back-to-back
narrative_blocks.body: He served as first Governor of Connecticut in 1639, alternating as governor and deputy governor eight separate terms until his death. Co-drafter of the Fundamental Orders (called the first written constitution, 1638/9). Invested £7,000-8,000 of his English fortune in Connecticut "to the ruine of his famylye in Englande."

narrative_blocks.header: His granddaughter Sarah married Yale's co-founder
narrative_blocks.body: His son Joseph became pastor of Hartford's First Church. Joseph's daughter Sarah Haynes married Rev. James Pierpont Sr. in 1694 — who secured the Yale charter in 1701. Sarah died October 27, 1696, the same day their daughter Abigail was born.

narrative_blocks.header: He governed Massachusetts before following Hooker west
narrative_blocks.body: Haynes served a year as governor of Massachusetts Bay in 1635 before leaving for the Connecticut River. He presided over the banishment of Roger Williams, a judgment he is said to have later regretted.

narrative_blocks.header: He spent an English fortune building Connecticut
narrative_blocks.body: Haynes sank £7,000 to £8,000 of his English estate into the colony — by his son's account, "to the ruine of his famylye in Englande." He helped form the New England Confederation in 1643.

death.cause: epidemic — died summer 1655, same as second husband Samuel Eaton

parents.father_research_notes: Of Staple Inn, London and Earls Colne, Essex. 1631 will mentions Mabel.

narrative_blocks.header: She sailed on the Defense with her brother
narrative_blocks.body: Mabel Harlakenden crossed to Massachusetts aboard the ship Defense in July 1635, with her brother Lt. Col. Roger Harlakenden. A portrait of her had been painted in England just before the voyage.

narrative_blocks.header: Widowed, she married Rev. Samuel Eaton
narrative_blocks.body: After Gov. John Haynes died, Mabel married Rev. Samuel Eaton in November 1654. She died the following June, in 1655.
